剧集名称: TED Talk - How would you finish the sentence,Imagine if...
对话文本: [Man] You know, there are very few things that set us apart from the rest of life on Earth. At the heart of them, I believe, is our an exhaustive power of imagination. Imagination is the ability to bring into mind things that aren't present. The imagination we can anticipate the future. We can revisit the past. We can step outside the present moment and try and see things that other people see them. It's through creativity that we develop our architecture, our scientific proofs, and our works of art. We don't just live in the world as we find it, we create civilizations, theories, technologies, and we reach beyond the planet. [Woman]In honor of the life and work of Sir Ken Robinson, Ted Ed invites you to reimagine the aspects of life we take for granted by finishing the sentence. Imagine if.
片段剧情描述: 来自《TED Talk - How would you finish the sentence,Imagine if...》，Sir Ken Robinson阐述想象力是人类区别于其他生物的核心能力，为了纪念他的研究，TED-ED网站邀请听众以“Imagine if”开头重新构想生活。

剧集名称: TED Talk - Online learning could change academia for good
对话文本: [Man] I suppose I inherited a love of teaching from my dad, and I planned to be a professor, just like him. I headed to grad school to do a PhD studying microbiology and biochemistry. But along the way, my dad's path and mine started to diverge. During long days in lab, I got impatient. I wanted to teach. I didn't want to wait to graduate and do a postdoc spend years navigating a treacherous job market. So I turned to the internet, which was breaking down the barriers to entry and removing the traditional gatekeepers in many established fields. From my kitchen table, I started recording tutorial videos, teaching the basics of chemistry using simple hand-drawn diagrams. And I put the videos on YouTube. And people actually started watching them. First a few folks, and then more, and then more. Thank you emails poured into my inbox. I wasn't a professor yet. I hadn't even finished my PhD. But I was helping students all over the world get through their chemistry classes.
片段剧情描述: 这是来自《TED Talk - Online learning could change academia for good》中的一段演讲：男性演讲者讲述自己受父亲影响热爱教学，但放弃传统学术道路，转而通过YouTube录制化学教学视频，帮助全球学生。

剧集名称: TED Talk - The world's biggest battery looks nothing like a battery
对话文本: [Man] As of 2020, the world's biggest lithium-ion battery is hooked up to the Southern California power grid and can provide 250 million watts of power, or enough to power about 250,000 homes. But it's actually not the biggest battery in the world. These lakes are. Wait, how can a pair of lakes be a battery? To answer that question, it helps to define a battery. It's simply something that stores energy and releases it on demand. The lithium-ion batteries that power our phones, laptops and cars are just one type. They store energy in lithium ions. To release the energy, the ions are separated from their electrons, then rejoined at the other end of the battery as a new molecule with lower energy. How do the two lakes store and release energy? First, one is 300 meters higher than the other. Electricity powers pumps that move billions of liters of water from the lower lake to the higher one. This stores the energy by giving the water extra gravitational potential energy. Then, when there's high demand for electricity, valves open, releasing the stored energy by letting water flow downhill to power six giant turbines that can generate 3 billion watts of power per 10 hours.
片段剧情描述: 这是来自《TED Talk - The world's biggest battery looks nothing like a battery》中的一段科普，演讲者解释世界上最大的电池并非锂离子电池，而是一对湖泊，通过抽水蓄能发电。

剧集名称: How does our immune system work?
对话文本:
[Man] The system relies on millions of defensive white blood cells, also known as leukocytes that originate in our bone marrow.
[Man] These cells migrate into the bloodstream and the lymphatic system, a network of vessels which helps clear bodily toxins and waste.
[Man] Our bodies are teaming with leukocytes. There are between 4,000 and 11,000 in every microliter of blood.
[Man] As they move around, leukocytes work like security personnel, constantly screening the blood, tissues, and organs for suspicious signs.
[Man] This system mainly relies on cues called antigens. These molecular traces on the surface of pathogens and other foreign substances betray the presence of invaders.
[Man] As soon as the leukocytes detect them, it takes only minutes for the body's protective immune response to kick in.
[Man] Threats to our bodies are hugely variable, so the immune response has to be equally adaptable.
[Man] That means relying on many different types of leukocytes to tackle threats in different ways.
片段剧情描述: 科普视频介绍免疫系统工作原理：白细胞巡逻、抗原识别及适应性免疫反应。

[Father] And what's it about?
[Son] I'm writing an article on the feelings about graduation.
[Father] And how do you feel?
[Son] Me? A little scared and excited, too.
[Father] I felt the same way. The scary part is leaving home and moving to college.
[Son] Oh.
[Father] Leaving home is part of growing up. Well, don't work all night.
[Son] I don't mind. I enjoy writing.
[Father] Well, maybe you should think about becoming a writer.
[Son] Maybe I should.
[Father] You have lots of time to decide.
[Son] That's the worst part, making decisions.
[Father] You'll be OK. Good night, son.
[Son] Good night, Dad.

People who make less money have children. After all, you always stay at home. [Woman]I don't just stay at home, Roman. I want to find myself. Don't compare me with other people. And besides, you knew that I wasn't going to be the type to do laundry and... and cook all day for you. I'm a creative being. I've been here painting all morning. Don't you like it? Or do you not like it? No, tell me if you don't like it. [Man]Tanya, I like everything you do. I really do. You're the most... talented, the most beautiful. Taste this. [Woman]Delicious. Very tasty. [Man]You're my one and only. [Woman]That's why I chose you, my dear. You're very patient. Please, you have to understand. It's very important for your wife to find herself in her art. [Man]Tanya, I gotta go. Okay? It's time now.
片段剧情描述: 这段对话出自《我的父亲是间谍》：丈夫罗曼憧憬未来有孩子、一起玩纸飞机，妻子塔尼娅则坚持先为自己而活，想先在艺术上找到自我。两人就“要不要现在生孩子”产生了分歧。

剧集名称: TED Talk - The exploitation of US college athletes
对话文本: [Man] In college sports, American universities are exploiting disproportionately black athletes for billions of dollars while diminishing their education, health, and safety. Let me start with a bit of history. In November 1984, an undersized quarterback from Boston College named Doug Flutey through a game-winning touchdown pass against the defending national champions, University of Miami. As the Hail Mary Pass floated through the fall air in front of a pack stadium, millions more watched with excitement on TV. After the dramatic win, undergraduate application rates at Boston College shot up by 30% revealing to universities the enormous marketing value of building high-profile sports programs. That same year, the United States Supreme Court heard a case in which the universities of Georgia and Oklahoma challenged rules that limited number of football games they could play on TV. Those schools saw the opportunity to not only make money by televising their games, but to also market their universities to the world. The Supreme Court agreed that the broadcasting restrictions were illegal and schools began to negotiate TV deals worth millions. That case opened the floodgates to money in college athletics and with it, ever growing conflicts of interest that prioritized sports over education, promote wins over health and safety, and reinforce the disturbing racial and economic inequities in our country. Since...
片段剧情描述: 这是来自《TED Talk - The exploitation of US college athletes》中的一段演讲，演讲者指出美国大学利用黑人运动员牟利，牺牲他们的教育、健康和安全，并回顾了1984年历史事件和最高法院案例如何导致大学体育商业化及种族经济不平等加剧。

剧集名称: TED Talk - The search for microscopic aliens
对话文本: [Woman] I want to find aliens. Finding life on another planet is not just going to be a little hard. It's going to be very hard. But for the first time in human history, we have a chance to detect signs of life on another planet. Or maybe we've already detected it on Venus. Or maybe not. There are still two big hurdles when it comes to confirming life on another world. The first is building a telescope big enough to do this. And the second is interpreting what we will find. When we think of extraterrestrial life, we tend to think of aliens like funny little green men, not aliens as single-celled microbes. But it's actually detecting signs of microbial life on another planet that I'm most optimistic about and what I focus my research on. I model how a star's high energy radiation can make gases from microbes harder or easier to see with future telescopes. Microids have dominated our planet's biosphere for most of Earth's history. They've been emitting gases that can be seen in our atmosphere even light years away for billions of years. Now if an alien astronomer were looking at Earth, they would probably detect gases like oxygen, methane, and nitrous oxide before detecting signs of us, even with an active biosphere like Earth. Most of the gases that indicate life are coming from single-celled microbes, not from animals. This is what we'll try to do in the next decade of astronomy. Try to find signs of microbial life on planets orbiting other stars.
片段剧情描述: 这是来自《TED Talk - The search for microscopic aliens》中的一段对话，演讲者讲述寻找外星微生物的挑战与希望，强调通过探测微生物气体信号来发现地外生命。

剧集名称: The journey of food through the digestive tract
对话文本: [Man] Across the whole planet, humans eat on average between 1.2 kilograms of food a day. That's over 365 kilograms a year per person, and more than 28,800 kilograms over the course of a lifetime. And every last scrap makes its way through the digestive system. Comprised of 10 organs covering 9 meters and containing over 20 specialized cell types, this is one of the most diverse and complicated systems in the human body. Its parts continuously work in unison to fulfill a singular task, transforming the raw materials of your food into the nutrients and energy that keep you alive. Spanning the entire length of your torso, the digestive system has four main components. First, there's the gastrointestinal tract, a twisting channel that transports your food and has an internal surface area of between 30 and 40 square meters, enough to cover half a badminton cord. Second, there's the pancreas, gallbladder, and liver, a trio of organs that break down food using an array of special juices. Third, the body's enzymes, hormones, nerves, and blood all work together to break down food, modulate the digestive process, and deliver its final products. Finally, there's the mezzantery, a large stretch of tissue that supports and positions all your digestive organs in the abdomen, enabling them to do their jobs. The digestive system is a very important part of the digestive system.
片段剧情描述: 旁白介绍人类每日平均摄入1.2公斤食物，消化系统由10个器官组成，总长9米，负责将食物转化为营养和能量，并概述其四大组成部分。

剧集名称: TED Talk - How to stop feeling anxious about anxiety
对话文本:
[Man] I started this talk by telling you I used to suffer debilitating levels of social anxiety.
[Man] So how did I get control of that? What did I actually do to reduce it?
[Man] There were three main things I did that I think helped me change.
[Man] The first thing I did, I refused to believe I was ill.
[Man] Of all the things I felt anxious about, I decided not to feel anxious about feeling anxious.
[Man] I recognized every time I felt anxiety, it wasn't random.
[Man] It was always triggered by a situation or a thought process.
[Man] Once I understood this, I could do the second thing.
[Man] That's to listen to my crew.
[Man] I spent too long pushing them away, trying to ignore what they were telling me.
[Man] When you ignore someone with an important message, they might just start to speak louder to get heard.
[Man] If you continue to ignore them, they might start to shout.
[Man] The only way I was going to stop anxiety from shouting at me was to listen to it, to hear what it was saying to me.
[Man] I sought to understand what my crew were trying to achieve with these feelings and to turn my enemies into my friends.
[Man] And the funny thing is, when you start listening to what your mind's trying to tell you, you can stop shouting.
[Man] When the crew stops shouting, the captain can be heard.
[Man] That's the point where your more logical thinking gets to have some input.
[Man] You get to decide, is this feeling appropriate or inappropriate? Is it helpful or unhelpful?
[Man] Once you have the ear of your crew and you know what you would want from them, the third thing, maybe the most important thing, is to always, always be kind to them.
[Man] If you're anything like me, you spent a large part of your life beating yourself up over the things you did wrong, or the things you didn't quite do right, or just about the way you feel.
[Man] For all the criticism and negativity you've received from others throughout your life, the person who's given you the most is probably you.
[Man] This has the cumulative effect of destroying morale aboard your ship.
[Man] A demoralized crew equals low self-esteem.
[Man] And low self-esteem means now the slightest challenge will create anxiety.
片段剧情描述: 演讲者分享克服社交焦虑的三个方法：不因焦虑而焦虑、倾听内心的声音（船员）、善待自己（船员）。通过理解焦虑触发因素，将敌人变为朋友，提升自尊。

对话文本:
[Harry] You like living in New York, don't you?
[Woman] Oh, I love it. It's so convenient. I can take the bus to work, or the subway, or a taxi, and there's so much to do. Lots of movie houses and the theater.
[Harry] I know what you mean. I'd like to live in a city. But living in New Jersey and the suburbs is better from a shell. Trees, grass. There's a lot of good things about suburban living. I grew up in Riverdale, remember? So I know.
[Woman] But as a working woman, I think New York has all the conveniences, including the best tomato.
[Harry] Hmm. The truth is, I'd like to live in a city. Michelle's the right age. There are lots of things for her here.
[Woman] You're right, Harry. Today is the perfect example. Michelle and her friends are at the Aquarium in Brooklyn. They come back here for lunch. Then go uptown to the Museum of Natural History. There's so much for young people to see and do. It's just incredible.
[Harry] Not just for young people. What about me? I've never been to the Aquarium, or the Museum of Natural History. Have you?
[Woman] Oh, yes, Harry. My mother and father often took us somewhere in the city on the weekends. Dad was a busy doctor, but he usually managed to squeeze a Sunday in with Richard Robby and me. I used to love to go to the Metropolitan Museum of Art.
[Harry] I've been there several times, twice with Michelle. You ought to think about spending more time with Michelle at all the great places in the city.
[Woman] Well, perhaps you will help me select some of the great places. And perhaps you'll join us?
[Harry] Perhaps I will.
片段剧情描述: Harry和一位女性讨论城市与郊区生活的优劣，女性提到自己成长经历，Harry邀请她一起带女儿Michelle游览纽约。

No queen either. Well, we make our own mattresses that don't conform to the traditional sizes. Closest would be twin. Thank you so much for calling. Call back again. I'll feed you the same.
[Jim] Hey, what? 
[Dwight] None of your business, Jim. You run the bed in breakfast? It's not a B&B. Agri-Turism is a lot more than a bed in breakfast. It consists of tourists coming to a farm, showing off their own food. Giving them a bed, giving them breakfast.
